explicit_text: I enlisted primarily because of the Attack on Pearl Harbor. I remember that day clearly in December 1941; I was coming down the steps of the old Montevilla Methodist Church in Portland when I heard from a friend listening to the radio that Pearl Harbor had just been bombed. That event was the catalyst for me joining the Navy and starting my military career.

I remember that day clearly in December 1941; I was coming down the steps of the old Montevilla Methodist Church in Portland when I heard from a friend listening to the radio that the base had just been bombed. That event was the catalyst for me joining the Navy and starting my military career.

explicit_text: I was living in Portland, Oregon when the Attack on Pearl Harbor happened. I recall it was a bright, sunny day as I was leaving Montevilla Methodist Church. Portland was also where I attended Multnomah Junior College, which was located in the YMCA building downtown. It was my home base before I enlisted in the Navy and later went off to other places for training and service.

explicit_text: I remember coming down the steps of the old Montevilla Methodist Church in Portland on the day Pearl Harbor was attacked. That building is no longer there, but it was at the same place. It was a significant moment because it was right there that I first heard the news of the attack, which influenced my decision to enlist in the Navy shortly afterward.

explicit_text: In April 1942, I enlisted in the V-6 Navy College Training Program, which was designed for college students as a pre-officers program. The Navy allowed me to stay in college for another year at Oregon State University while being part of this program. It was through V-6 that I received my officer training and eventually was commissioned as a supply officer.

explicit_text: I served in the United States Navy during World War II, enlisting in 1942 after Pearl Harbor. I was part of the V-6 program, trained at Purdue University and Wellesley College, and commissioned as a supply officer. My service included assignments at Solomons, Maryland, Galveston, Texas, Panama, and San Diego, California. Though I never saw combat, I handled pay and record-keeping duties until my discharge in 1946.

I was part of the college training program, trained at universities in Indiana and Massachusetts, and commissioned as a supply officer. My service included assignments at Solomons, Maryland, Galveston, Texas, Panama, and San Diego, California. Though I never saw combat, I handled pay and record-keeping duties until my discharge in 1946.

explicit_text: After enlisting in the V-6 program, the Navy allowed me to spend another year at Oregon State University in 1942. I used the money I earned working as a welder at Kaiser Shipyards to pay for my tuition there. Oregon State was an important step in my education before I went on active duty with the Navy.

explicit_text: Before joining the Navy, I was attending Multnomah Junior College in Portland, which was housed in the YMCA building downtown. While there, I took welding classes in their industrial division to prepare for work in the shipyards. This training helped me secure a summer job at Kaiser Shipyards before moving on to Oregon State University.

explicit_text: During the summer before I went to Oregon State, I worked as a welder at Kaiser Shipyards. I had to pass several welding tests, including flat, vertical, and overhead positions, which was challenging. I worked long hours, sometimes waking up at 4:30 a.m. for classes before my shift, and earned $2.50 an hour, which was a good wage then. I saved over a thousand dollars to fund my college education.

explicit_text: After being called to active duty in July 1943, I went to the Boston Receiving Station. While there, I attended Navy officer training at Wellesley College, which is near Boston. I also spent a Christmas at a local family's home in the Boston area during my training, which was a memorable experience.

While there, I attended Navy officer training at a nearby women's college. I also spent a Christmas at a local family's home in that area during my training, which was a memorable experience.

explicit_text: In the fall of 1943, the Navy sent me to Purdue University in Indiana as part of my midshipman training. We lived in a fraternity house and took courses like military German, navigation, and spherical trigonometry. I met a fellow midshipman named George there, who was an agricultural student and later became a successful farmer.

explicit_text: After Purdue, I attended Navy Officers School at Wellesley College, a women's college near Boston. The Navy midshipmen were housed in one part of a quadrangle, while the women lived in the other parts. I was commissioned as a supply officer after several months there, and it was at Wellesley that I had one of my memorable Christmas dinners with a local family.

explicit_text: After commissioning, I was assigned with Al Benson to a fleet of 36 Landing Craft, Infantry (Large), or LCILs. I took the role of disbursing officer while Benson handled supply. We trained at Solomons, Maryland, and later gathered our flotilla near Galveston, Texas. The LCILs were low-decked ships, and during one trip to Panama, our commodore even stopped the ship so we could swim.
implicit_text: After commissioning, I was assigned with Al Benson to a fleet of 36 large infantry landing craft. I took the role of disbursing officer while Benson handled supply. We trained at Solomons, Maryland, and later gathered our flotilla near Galveston, Texas. The landing craft were low-decked ships, and during one trip to Panama, our commodore even stopped the ship so we could swim.

explicit_text: I spent the summer training at Solomons, Maryland with the LCIL fleet. It was very hot and humid, quite a change from the Northwest climate I was used to. We taught stores officers there, who were commissioned but not supply corps officers, how to manage supply and disbursing duties on small ships.

explicit_text: We were assigned to a Navy base near Galveston, Texas, where we gathered our flotilla of 36 LCILs. From there, we had orders to proceed to various locations, including a trip to Chicago for some of us. The time in Galveston was part of our preparation before heading out to other assignments.

explicit_text: Al Benson lived in Chicago, so when we had proceed orders, we went there for a short time. The weather was cold compared to the hot summer in Maryland, and after returning south, I caught pneumonia. Chicago was a brief but significant stop during my Navy service.

explicit_text: When I got pneumonia after returning from Chicago, there was no Navy hospital available, so I was treated in a United States Army hospital. This took me out of active duty with my LCIL team and led to surgeries and a reassignment. The Army hospital care was an unexpected part of my Navy service experience.

explicit_text: After recovering from pneumonia and surgeries, I was assigned to the Receiving Station in San Diego, California. My main duty was paying sailors who had been at sea for long periods and hadn't been paid, as well as handling pay at a nearby Marine base. I remained on land in San Diego for the rest of my service and never went to sea again.

I remained on land in this city for the rest of my service and never went to sea again.

explicit_text: I spent a short time in Panama during my Navy service, traveling there on an LCIL. We stopped for swimming during the trip under the watch of a commodore who was relaxed and approachable. I traveled by train across the isthmus on an old train rather than passing through the Panama Canal by ship. Later, I was taken from Panama to San Diego on a cruiser, wearing a life preserver due to medication.

This was part of my administrative role during the latter part of my service.

explicit_text: The officer in charge of our LCIL fleet held the rank of commodore, which was uncommon. He was a Naval Academy veteran who had retired but returned to service during the war. He was a down-to-earth leader who even stopped the ship so we could go swimming, posting men on shark watch. His relaxed style was memorable and different from typical Navy formality.

explicit_text: My sister served in the Navy as part of the WAVES, though she was not an officer. I wrote to her regularly during the war. Having family in the WAVES gave me a personal connection to the Navy beyond my own service.

explicit_text: After my discharge from the Navy in 1946, I enrolled at Willamette University College of Law in September of that year. I had completed three years of undergraduate work and finished the last year during summer school. I graduated in 1949 with both a law degree and a B.A., starting my civilian career after military service.
